summary: Joel Spolsky provides twelve practical guidelines for running effective beta tests of shrinkwrap software products. He argues that open betas are ineffective and that structured, application-based beta programs yield far better feedback. The post emphasizes realistic timelines (minimum eight weeks, four releases), the psychology of getting testers to actually submit feedback, and the math behind how many testers you need. He also warns against adding features during beta and distinguishes between technical betas (for finding bugs) and marketing betas (for press and partners).
key_points: Open betas fail because they produce either too many testers to manage or too few useful reports; closed application-based betas leverage consistency psychology to get real feedback
Beta timelines have hard minimums: eight weeks, four releases, no more than one build every two weeks, and adding any feature resets the clock
Only about one in five approved beta testers will actually provide meaningful feedback, so you must over-recruit by 5x to hit your target of serious testers
Stagger beta tester groups across releases to ensure fresh eyes on each build, since most testers lose interest after their first try
Technical betas for finding bugs and marketing betas for press and partners serve fundamentally different purposes and should not be conflated
money_quotes: Open betas don't work. You either get too many testers in which case you can't get good data from the testers, or too few reports from the existing testers.
The best way to get a beta tester to send you feedback is to appeal to their psychological need to be consistent.
Don't think you can get through a full beta cycle in less than eight to ten weeks. I've tried; lord help me, it just can't be done.
If you add a feature, even a small one, during the beta process, the clock goes back to the beginning of the eight weeks and you need another 3-4 releases.
Even if you have an application process, only about one in five people will send you feedback anyway.
Most beta testers will try out the program when they first get it, and then lose interest.
Don't confuse a technical beta with a marketing beta.
key_insight: Effective beta testing requires structured recruitment that leverages consistency psychology, realistic timelines of at least eight weeks with four staggered releases, and significant over-recruitment since only one in five testers will actually provide useful feedback.
